name: Thorn's Reversal
description: Stone has the reversal technique from the Hall of Rememberers. The Ninth can perform it. Thorn has agreed — in the way he agrees to things, which is not gracefully. The Hall is waiting.
accept_text: Stone is at the research table she's been working at for three days straight — the Hall's original documentation spread across it, handwritten copies of two decades of Elder technique, the kind of work she was built for before the war got in the way of what she was supposed to be. She doesn't look up immediately. 'Thorn said yes.' She says it to the documentation. 'He took two days. He said he needed to think. He came to me on the third morning and told me he had a list of objections and that none of them were good enough.' She finally looks up. 'He said: I told Serel I would try to be here. And this is the trying.' She is quiet for a moment. 'I have the complete technique. The Ninth has confirmed the process — it's the same one the Watcher used in the years after the Cascade, the same one that reversed the early integrations when subjects changed their minds. I have run the parameters four times. The growth rate in Thorn's case means the reversal window is narrow but present.' She closes the documentation. 'The Hall is ready. The Ninth is ready. I am ready.' She pauses. 'I would like you to be there. You've been in this particular thread since the beginning, and — I want a witness who isn't me.'
complete_text: The Hall of Rememberers holds still for this. The Ninth speaks from the deep crystal — the resonance that is not words but is understood as words, the translation that the Elder People built into the network. It describes what it is doing in real time as it does it, not for Thorn but for Stone, who needs to understand every step for the documentation she'll write afterward. Thorn sits in the center of the chamber and does not speak. His face has the specific quality of someone who has decided not to narrate what is happening to them. The process takes forty minutes. It is not violent and it is not comfortable and it is not what anyone would call dramatic. The crystals under the skin of his forearms — the slow blue growth that has been advancing for three years — stop. Then, by increments so gradual you think you might be imagining it, they begin to recede. Stone is watching instruments and taking notes and has been crying since the twenty-minute mark without acknowledging it. When the Ninth says the process is complete Thorn holds up his arms and looks at the skin above the wrists for a long time. 'Still there,' he says. Not sad. Factual. 'Stone says six months before it's gone.' He looks at her. 'I was going to die in that command post anyway. Might as well be upright when I do it.' Stone sets down her pen. She looks at him for a moment that contains twenty years of complicated professional history and the specific grief of someone who built a thing that hurt people and has just, for once, built something that helped the specific person it needed to help. She says nothing. She picks up the pen. She keeps working.
